title: Quiz: Circuit Complexity
description: Test your understanding of quantum circuit complexity measures including gate count and circuit depth

questions.question: What is the primary difference between gate complexity and circuit depth?

options.text: Gate complexity counts only single-qubit gates, depth counts all gates
Gate complexity is total gate count, depth is the number of sequential layers
Gate complexity uses Clifford gates, depth uses T gates
Gate complexity is approximate, depth is exact

questions.explanation: Gate complexity counts the total number of gates used, while circuit depth counts the number of sequential layers where parallel gates count as one layer. A circuit with many parallel gates can have high gate complexity but low depth.

questions.question: According to the Solovay-Kitaev theorem, how many gates are needed to approximate a single-qubit unitary to precision epsilon?
questions.explanation: The Solovay-Kitaev theorem states that O(log^c(1/epsilon)) gates suffice, where c is approximately 3.97. This polylogarithmic scaling is remarkably efficient, though modern methods achieve even better constants.

questions.question: What is the minimum number of CNOT gates required to implement a generic two-qubit unitary?
questions.explanation: Any two-qubit unitary can be implemented with at most 3 CNOTs and single-qubit gates (via KAK decomposition). Some operations like SWAP require exactly 3 CNOTs, proving this bound is tight.

questions.question: If a circuit has gate complexity G and width W, its depth must be at least G/W.
questions.explanation: True. Since at most W gates can execute in parallel (one per qubit), you need at least G/W layers to execute all G gates. This is the fundamental depth-width tradeoff.

questions.question: Why is T-complexity particularly important for fault-tolerant quantum computing?

options.text: T gates are the fastest to execute on hardware
T gates are the only non-Clifford gates
T gates require expensive magic state distillation
T gates have the smallest error rates

questions.explanation: In fault-tolerant quantum computing, Clifford gates can be implemented transversally (cheaply), but T gates require magic state distillation, which is expensive in terms of physical resources. Thus, T-count dominates the cost.

questions.question: What does a counting argument tell us about the circuit complexity of generic n-qubit unitaries?

options.text: All can be done in polynomial gates

questions.explanation: The unitary group U(2^n) has dimension 4^n. Since a circuit with k gates can only implement polynomially many distinct unitaries in k, most unitaries require Omega(4^n / poly(n)) gates. Most unitaries are exponentially complex.

questions.question: Which quantum complexity class contains problems solvable by polynomial-size quantum circuits?
questions.explanation: BQP (Bounded-error Quantum Polynomial time) is the class of problems solvable by polynomial-size quantum circuits with bounded error probability. It is the quantum analogue of BPP.

questions.question: Which factors make circuit depth critical for near-term quantum computing? (Select all that apply)

options.text: Deeper circuits accumulate more decoherence errors
Depth determines execution time on hardware
Current hardware has limited coherence times

questions.explanation: Circuit depth is critical because: (a) each layer allows more decoherence, (b) depth equals time on parallel hardware, and (d) current quantum computers have limited coherence times. Depth does NOT directly affect qubit count (that's width).
